content:
3 min read
Just now

--

It’s the end of another week.
I know this feeling well. The pull to do something useful before the next one starts. The quiet hope that if I set something up tonight, Monday will go differently. I’ve been chasing that feeling for as long as I can remember.
This summer, something finally shifted.
For ten years, I’ve been writing workbooks. One topic at a time, one question I was quietly trying to answer for myself. How do I build a habit that actually sticks? What does real discipline look like when no one is watching? How do I get honest about what I want and then actually go get it?
I wasn’t writing for an audience. I was just putting it on the page.
Each workbook lived on its own. I’d finish one, set it aside, start another. I wasn’t thinking about how they fit together. I wasn’t thinking about a system. I was just trying to figure things out, one piece at a time, the only way I know how: by writing it down.
Then this summer I did something I’d never done before. I laid them all out. Not to organize them, just to see them. All of it, together, for the first time.
I wasn’t looking at a collection.
I was looking at a system.
Not because all of them belonged together. Some of what I’ve built over the years is good on its own. But it doesn’t connect to the others, it doesn’t build on what comes before or set up what comes next. I could see that clearly now. And so I made choices. Not everything made the cut. The pieces that did were chosen intentionally, because together they do something that none of them can do alone.
That’s what makes it a system. Not the quantity. The intention.
There’s a word for that: curated. I’d never applied it to my own work before. But that’s what this is. A curated set of tools, built one at a time over a decade, that only revealed what they were when I finally looked at all of them at once.
I had to sit with that for a while.
Because here’s what it also meant: I had been building this for myself. Every workbook I wrote was me trying to solve something I hadn’t solved yet. The habits work was for the version of me who couldn’t make things stick. The discipline section was for the version of me who kept starting over. The goals work was for the version of me who had a vague sense of direction but no real plan.
I wrote my way through those versions of myself. And at some point — I don’t know exactly when — it started working.
That’s when a question I’d been quietly ignoring got loud enough to answer:
How many more Sundays would pass before I did something with this?
Not a dramatic question. Not a crisis. Just a quiet, honest reckoning. The kind that comes when you finally see something clearly and realize you’ve seen it long enough.
The thing about a system is that it changes how a Sunday feels. Not because it eliminates the pull — that’s part of who I am, and honestly I’ve made peace with it. But because when you know where something fits, even 20 minutes means something. You’re not just doing one more thing. You’re taking a step in something that actually moves.
That’s the difference. That’s what I finally had.
Next week, I’ll show you what it looks like.
— -
This week’s Small Move:
Take ten minutes and write down three things you’ve been slowly building, in any area of your life. Don’t evaluate them. Just list them. Then ask yourself: Do these connect?Is there a thread? Sometimes the system shows up before you have a name for it.
